S1
Chapter One
Lost in MacRitchie — The Day Police Rescued Me From the Forest
🌿 Singapore  ·  April 10, 2023

On April 10, 2023, my friend and I got completely lost inside MacRitchie Nature Trail and Reservoir Park. What followed was a rescue operation involving the Singapore Police Force, the Gurkha Contingent, the Aerial Response Team, and officers from NParks and PUB. We made the national news.

It started simply enough. My friend, aged 51, and I decided to go for a hike. We left my house at 8am feeling confident and well-prepared — or so we thought. MacRitchie is a well-known nature reserve in the heart of Singapore. Families go there. School children go there. It did not seem dangerous.

We did not bring enough water. We did not have offline maps. We did not tell anyone exactly which trail we were taking. Three classic mistakes — and we made all three before 9am.

At 3pm, we reached the Ruins of Syonan Jinja — the old Japanese shrine deep inside the reserve. It was an interesting landmark — but we had a serious problem. We could not find the trail back out. Every path looked the same. The jungle was dense, the light was fading, and my phone battery was at 12%.

We tried three different paths. Each time, we ended up back at the same spot. We were going in circles. That was the moment I admitted to myself: we are genuinely lost.

Then it started to rain heavily. We were soaked, exhausted, and completely off the trail. My friend's battery was dead. Mine was barely alive. We called 999.

"Within the hour: Singapore Police Force · Gurkha Contingent · Aerial Response Team · NParks · PUB — all deployed. It became a full search-and-rescue operation broadcast on national news."

The Singapore Police Force deployed officers from multiple units. A rescue boat came through the reservoir. The Aerial Response Team swept overhead with drones. We were found at dusk — cold, embarrassed, but completely safe. The officers were professional and kind. They did not scold us.

MacRitchie taught me that overconfidence is the most dangerous thing you can bring into a forest. I am 70 years old. I still hike. I still explore. But now I do it smarter — with offline maps, a fully charged power bank, and a healthy respect for nature.

"The forest does not care how many countries you have visited. It will humble you every time."

S3
Chapter Three
The Day I Lost My iPhone in Portugal
📱 Portugal  ·  June 23, 2025

How did a spare phone, a local SIM card, and two helpful Portuguese police officers turn a disaster into a story worth telling?

On 23 June 2025, I was travelling solo in Portugal. At seventy, I still prefer hostels and public buses to package tours — they keep the trip honest and the wallet light. That afternoon, I boarded a Rede Expressos bus in Albufeira. It was the 14:45 service to Beja — just a short ride through the Portuguese countryside.

Somewhere along the way, the bus pulled into a roadside stop. I rushed to the toilet, then bought an ice cream from a small stall. I sat down at a wooden table in the shade and enjoyed it — the kind of simple pleasure that makes solo travel feel good at seventy. When the driver called everyone back, I stood up, finished the last bite, and climbed onto the bus. I did not look back at the table.

The bus had already pulled onto the highway when my hand reached into my pocket and found nothing. I checked the other pocket. My bag. The seat beside me. Nothing. My new iPhone — all my photos, bookings, contacts, maps — was gone.

"At seventy, you learn that panic does not help anything. But knowing that does not always stop the panic from coming."

The Spare Phone That Saved the Day

I always travel with a spare phone in my bag for exactly these situations. I pulled it out and started thinking. First, I needed a local SIM for mobile data. After arriving in Beja, I found a small shop and bought one.

The Find My app showed a green dot with a precise address: Praceta Jaime Cortesão 5, 7800-028 Beja. Someone had picked up my iPhone at the rest stop and taken it home. Because the phone had an eSIM — built into the phone, impossible to remove with a pin — it kept reporting its location. Quietly. Honestly. From inside someone else's flat.

I walked straight to the nearest police station. The officer looked at the map on my spare phone, nodded, and made a phone call. The next day, officers went to the flat. They knocked on the door. The person inside admitted he had picked it up. They returned it to me.

The streets of Beja, when I walked them again, looked different. Friendlier. Lighter.

The Algarve Coast — iPhone Back in Pocket

With my iPhone back in my pocket, I could finally enjoy the rest of the trip. I went out on a small boat ride along the Algarve coast — the yellow sandstone cliffs, the sea arches, the sea caves I had been looking forward to for months.

Sitting on that little boat, the water gently rocking under me, I thought to myself: I am seventy years old, alone in a foreign country, and I solved this myself. That feeling was worth more than the phone.

"Travel keeps me young because it keeps reminding me that I can still figure things out. The ice cream was good too."
